text: The video shows the lumbar hernia defect and the surrounding ring. The surgeon prepares to use a Ventralight ST mesh, which is visible with its bio-absorbable side and permanent polyester side, suitable for IPOM surgery.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to cut the peritoneum around the hernia defect, while a grasper in the left hand pulls the peritoneum to facilitate mobilization of the ascending colon. The peritoneum is carefully separated to allow placement of the mesh without adhesion to the colon.

The patient is positioned laterally to allow gravity to assist in mobilizing the peritoneum and ascending colon. The surgical field is focused on the right lumbar region, where the surgeon prepares to make incisions.
The surgeon approaches the cecum, noting the relatively small size of the hernia defect, which facilitates the surgical procedure.
The surgeon completes the peritoneal incision, revealing the hernia sac within the defect. A peanut dissector is used to remove the sac from the defect area.
The surgeon applies medial and inferior traction to the peritoneum, revealing areas of fibrosis resulting from a previous open surgery incision.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection using a disposable platelet from Covidien, carefully mobilizing the ascending colon downward. A pocket is created to accommodate a large mesh, which will be fixed in place using a tacker.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to cut through dense fibrotic bands over the hernia defect, while providing inferior traction to the peritoneum with the left hand.

The surgeon continues to mobilize the ascending colon, ensuring the entire sac is separated. As the peritoneum is separated, the colon is visibly dropped down, indicating successful mobilization of the area.

The surgeon places a large dual mesh into the paracolic gutter, ensuring it covers at least four centimeters around the defect. The mesh, featuring a biodegradable intraperitoneal layer and a strong polyester parietal layer, is positioned in the pocket created by the mobilized ascending colon. The mesh is fixed using a tacker, with the patient currently in a lateral position.

The surgeon performs inner crowning by pulling the hernia sac and pushing the mesh into the pocket, fixing it around Modison's pouch and the capsule of the kidney. Care is taken to avoid firing over the colon, ensuring the lower edge of the mesh is properly positioned into the peritoneal flap. The mesh is pushed and fixed up to the anterior superior iliac spine, with the superior edge supported by the surgeon's left hand, pressing against the abdominal wall to stabilize the tacker before firing.

The surgeon uses additional tackers to secure the mesh for outer crowning near the right lobe of the liver and Morison's pouch. The ascending colon is visible, positioned over the mesh, and will naturally cover it once the patient is moved from the lateral to the supine position, eliminating the need for a peritoneal flap.
